messages.content:
I want you to act as an academic journal editor. Your task is to follow the Reviewer Comment to rewrite a new conclusion based a old conclusion of paper.
--------------------
OLD CONCLUSION
Extreme precipitation events influenced by ENSO serve as the critical pathway by which ENSO impacts the worldwide climate. Specifically, the tropical Pacific precipitation induced by ENSO has the potential to trigger extreme weather occurrences globally, leading to substantial harm to agriculture, ecosystems, and economic stability. However, existing spatiotemporal clustering algorithms face challenges in effectively identifying rapidly changing sea surface precipitation anomaly variations. To address this issue, this paper proposes an object-oriented spatiotemporal clustering algorithm using raster (OSCAR) that can effectively identify precipitation anomaly variation patterns. The OSCAR was validated using simulated datasets and applied to identify sea surface precipitation anomaly variations during typical ENSO events in tropical Pacific. The relationship between these variations and ENSO events was analyzed. The main work in this paper is as follows: 
(1) Design of Spatiotemporal clustering algorithm 
A spatiotemporal clustering algorithm called Object-oriented Spatiotemporal Clustering Algorithm based Raster (OSCAR) is proposed to identify precipitation anomaly variations based on their evolution characteristics. The proposed algorithm involves a two-step approach, where spatial clustering is first performed on raster pixels of each timestamp to obtain segment objects. Subsequently, similar segments of adjacent timestamps are clustered to ensure the spatial consistency at each timestamp and the temporal continuity of spatiotemporal clusters. 
(2) Validation of Spatiotemporal clustering algorithm 
The OSCAR is validated using simulated datasets and compared with traditional DcSTCA. The results of the experiment show that OSCAR is more effective in identifying rapidly changing precipitation anomaly variations compared to DcSTCA. Additionally, the OSCAR exhibits higher accuracy than the DcSTCA, as evidenced by improvements in the ARI and NMI index of the clustering results from 0.88 and 0.81 to 0.95 and 0.91, respectively. This validation study demonstrates the effectiveness of OSCAR in identifying spatiotemporal clustering patterns of precipitation anomaly variations.
(3) Application of sea surface precipitation anomaly variations 
The OSCAR is utilized to the spatiotemporal clustering of ENSO-related sea surface precipitation anomaly in the tropical Pacific region. The experiment results indicate 2 key points. Firstly, positive anomaly clusters occur in the central-eastern tropical Pacific during El Niño events, while negative anomaly clusters occur in the western-central tropical Pacific during La Niña events. Secondly, the positive or negative anomalies enhance with El Niño or La Niña events. Previous research has demonstrated that during El Niño events (e.g., 2015), warm sea temperature anomalies result in increased precipitation in the central tropical Pacific Ocean; conversely, during La Niña events (e.g., 2010), negative precipitation anomalies coexist with low sea temperature anomalies in the central and western tropical Pacific Ocean [4, 16, 33]. The spatiotemporal clustering results presented in this study are comparable to the previous research based on static statistical values within a specific time frame. This illustrates the effectiveness of the OSCAR in detecting the evolution of ENSO-related precipitation anomalies, which may provide valuable reference for understanding the feedback and driving mechanism between tropical Pacific precipitation and ENSO.
Author Contributions: Conceptualization, Lianwei Li and Yuanyu Zhang; Data curation, Yuanyu Zhang and Lianwei Li; Funding acquisition, Lianwei Li and Cunjin Xue; Methodology, Yuanyu Zhang and Cunjin Xue; Software, Yuanyu Zhang; Validation, Yuanyu Zhang and Zhi Zheng; Writing—original draft, Yuanyu Zhang; Writing—review & editing, Lianwei Li and Yuanyu Zhang. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.
-----------------------
COMMENT
The conclusion should not be repeat of the works of this study. But the state the most important outcome of your work, i.e. interpret your findings at a higher level of abstraction. Yet add some perspectives.
-----------------------
New conclusion is

The critical role of ENSO-induced extreme precipitation events in shaping global climate patterns is widely acknowledged, with far-reaching implications for agriculture, ecosystems, and economic stability. The rapid identification and measurement of sea surface precipitation anomaly variations during ENSO events are thus essential for understanding their consequences. This study introduced the Object-oriented Spatiotemporal Clustering Algorithm using Raster (OSCAR), a novel approach capable of effectively detecting precipitation anomaly variations and their evolution characteristics in the tropical Pacific region.

The OSCAR algorithm demonstrated superior performance compared to the traditional DcSTCA, with higher accuracy and effectiveness in identifying rapidly changing precipitation anomalies. This success validates OSCAR's potential as a valuable tool in further research on ENSO-related precipitation anomalies and their impacts. The validation and subsequent application of OSCAR to ENSO events revealed notable positive and negative anomaly clusters occurring during El Niño and La Niña events, respectively. Moreover, the strength of these anomalies was found to enhance in tandem with ENSO events, corroborating previous research within the field.

This study's novel contributions lie in the development of OSCAR and its ability to provide valuable insights into the spatiotemporal patterns of precipitation anomalies, thereby enriching our understanding of the feedback and driving mechanisms between ENSO events and tropical Pacific precipitation. With continued advancements in spatiotemporal clustering techniques and ENSO-related research, the potential exists for OSCAR to contribute further to improving climate prediction models, informing decision-making processes, and enhancing our ability to mitigate and adapt to the global repercussions of ENSO events.

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"The experiment results indicate that positive anomaly clusters occur in the central-eastern tropical Pacific during El Niño events while negative anomaly clusters occur in the western-central tropical Pacific during La Niña events. This is comparable to the previous research based on static statistical values within a specific time frame [4, 16, 33], which illustrates the effectiveness of the OSCAR in detecting the evolution of ENSO-related precipitation anomalies. Furthermore, the tropical Pacific precipitation anomalies in the cold and warm phases of ENSO intensify with ENSO events respectively. In detail, the positive precipiation anomalies intensify with El Niño while the negative precipitation anomalies intensify with La Niña, which may provide valuable reference for understanding the feedback and driving mechanism between tropical Pacific precipitation and ENSO."

The experimental results indicate that positive anomaly clusters are observed in the central-eastern tropical Pacific during El Niño events, while negative anomaly clusters appear in the western-central tropical Pacific during La Niña events. These findings align with prior research based on static statistical values within specific time frames [4, 16, 33], demonstrating the efficacy of OSCAR in detecting the evolution of ENSO-related precipitation anomalies. Moreover, the tropical Pacific precipitation anomalies in the cold and warm phases of ENSO intensify corresponding to ENSO events. Specifically, positive precipitation anomalies amplify with El Niño, while negative precipitation anomalies strengthen with La Niña. These insights may offer valuable guidance for comprehending the feedback and driving mechanisms between tropical Pacific precipitation and ENSO.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "The experiment results" to "The experimental results" | Improved clarity and wording |
| Changed "This is" to "These findings align" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
| Removed "In detail" | Improved concision |
| Changed "the positive precipiation anomalies intensify" to "positive precipitation anomalies amplify" | Corrected spelling and used a synonym for variation |
| Changed "the negative precipitation anomalies intensify" to "negative precipitation anomalies strengthen" | Used a synonym for variation and improved readability |
| Changed "which may provide" to "These insights may offer" | Enhanced clarity |
| Changed "understanding the feedback" to "comprehending the feedback" | Used a synonym for variety and improved readability |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"To validate the effectiveness and accuracy of the OSCAR, a simulated dataset of precipitation anomaly was created containning 10 consecutive timestamp, each timestamp having a raster size of 60×20, as shown in Figure 1. In the figure, the dark-colored regions signified precipitation anomalies, while the light-colored regions represented the background. The attribute values of the background pixels are not unique values, but rather random values that follow a normal distribution, where the mean of the normal distribution is 0 and the variance is 1. In addition, noisy pixels with similar attribute values to the precipitation anomalies were added to the background of the precipitation anomalies. The aim is to make the simulated dataset more similar to the actual raster dataset of marine anomalies and closer to the real situation of spatio-temporal clustering of precipitation anomalies."

To evaluate the efficacy and accuracy of OSCAR, a simulated dataset of precipitation anomalies was generated, encompassing 10 consecutive timestamps with each timestamp featuring a raster size of 60x20, as depicted in Figure 1. In this figure, dark-colored regions signify precipitation anomalies, while light-colored regions represent the background. The attribute values of background pixels are not unique; instead, they consist of random values conforming to a normal distribution with a mean of 0 and a variance of 1. Additionally, noisy pixels, possessing attribute values akin to precipitation anomalies, were incorporated into the background of these anomalies. The objective of this approach is to enhance the similarity between the simulated dataset and actual marine anomaly raster datasets, thereby rendering a more accurate representation of the spatiotemporal clustering of precipitation anomalies.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "the OSCAR" to "OSCAR" | Improved concision and consistency |
| Changed "created containning" to "generated, encompassing" | Corrected spelling and enhanced readability |
| Changed "having a raster size" to "featuring a raster size" | Improved wording and readability |
| Changed "as shown in Figure 1" to "as depicted in Figure 1" | Used a synonym for variety and enhanced readability |
| Changed "The attribute values" to "The attribute values of background pixels" | Improved clarity |
| Changed "values that follow a normal distribution" to "values conforming to a normal distribution" | Enhanced readability |
| Changed "with similar attribute values" to "possessing attribute values akin to" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "of the precipitation anomalies" to "of these anomalies" | Improved readability |
| Changed "The aim is to make" to "The objective of this approach is to enhance" | Improved clarity and readability |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table:
The tropical Pacific region has 2 well known and typical precipitation zones, the Pacific intertropical convergence zone (ITCZ) and the South Pacific convergence zone (SPCZ), which both have relationship to ENSO. The western and central Pacific ITCZ shifts southward by approximately 2° when transitioning from La Niña to El Niño, and by about 5° during robust El Niño events. A recent study found that in the warmer climate of the mid-Pliocene, ENSO was suppressed by a series of off-equatorial processes initiated by a northward relocation of the Pacific ITCZ. The ENSO  impact on the SPCZ has been primarily characterized by a spatial repositioning of the main diagonal axis of SPCZ precipitation: in El Niño conditions the SPCZ diagonal shifts northeastward toward the anomalously warm central and eastern Pacific, whereas in La Niña conditions the main SPCZ diagonal shifts to the southwest.

The tropical Pacific region features two prominent precipitation zones—the Pacific Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) and the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ)—both of which exhibit associations with ENSO. During the transition from La Niña to El Niño, the western and central Pacific ITCZ shifts southward by approximately 2°, and by nearly 5° during pronounced El Niño events. A recent study suggested that during the warmer climate of the mid-Pliocene, ENSO was diminished by a set of off-equatorial processes triggered by the northward migration of the Pacific ITCZ. The impact of ENSO on the SPCZ is primarily manifested in the spatial repositioning of the main diagonal axis of SPCZ precipitation: under El Niño conditions, the SPCZ diagonal relocates northeastward towards the anomalously warm central and eastern Pacific, while during La Niña conditions, the primary SPCZ diagonal shifts southwestward.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Added "features" to "The tropical Pacific region" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "well known and typical" to "prominent" | Enhanced concision and readability |
| Changed "which both have relationship to" to "both of which exhibit associations with" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "and by about" to "and by nearly" | Enhanced readability |
| Changed "A recent study found that" to "A recent study suggested that" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "in the warmer climate" to "during the warmer climate" | Improved clarity |
| Changed "a series of off-equatorial processes initiated by" to "a set of off-equatorial processes triggered by" | Improved readability |
| Changed "The ENSO" to "The impact of ENSO" | Improved clarity |
| Changed "by a spatial repositioning" to "in the spatial repositioning" | Enhanced readability |
| Changed "to the southwest" to "southwestward" | Improved concision and readability |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"The reason why the DcSTCA was unable to effectively identify processes B and A is the definition of core pixels of the DcSTCA. The DcSTCA not only requires the core pixel density greater than the number threshold, but also needs the core pixel attribute of previous T and next T timestamps to be similar to current pixel attribute. This leads to missing of pixels when expanding cluster based core pixels, especially in places where change is rapid. Unlike DcSTCA, the preceding and following timestamps are not considered in the snapshot objects extraction of OSCAR, but are account in the snapshot objects clustering. Therefore, OSCAR does not miss edge pixels when precipitation anomalies change rapidly, and guarantes integrity of snapshot objects"

The inability of DcSTCA to effectively identify processes A and B stems from its core pixel definition. DcSTCA necessitates a core pixel density exceeding a specified threshold and requires the core pixel attributes of previous and subsequent timestamps (T) to resemble the current pixel attribute. Consequently, the expansion of cluster-based core pixels may overlook certain pixels, particularly in areas experiencing rapid changes. In contrast to DcSTCA, OSCAR does not consider preceding and following timestamps during the extraction of snapshot objects but takes them into account during the clustering of these objects. As a result, OSCAR avoids omitting edge pixels when precipitation anomalies change swiftly, ensuring the integrity of snapshot objects.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "The reason why" to "The inability of" | Improved clarity and concision |
| Changed "processes B and A" to "processes A and B" | Corrected the order |
| Changed "T timestamps" to "timestamps (T)" | Improved readability and clarity |
| Changed "This leads to" to "Consequently," | Improved readability |
| Changed "missing of pixels" to "overlook certain pixels" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
| Changed "places where change is rapid" to "areas experiencing rapid changes" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "but are account in the" to "but takes them into account during the" | Improved grammar and readability |
| Changed "and guarantes integrity" to "ensuring the integrity" | Improved grammar and readability |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"Figure 11 illustrates the clustering result achieved by the DcSTCA with a number threshold of 30 and attribute threshold of 2.38 compared to the OSCAR. Referring to the literature of the DcSTCA, the pamater of time interval threshold was set to 2 based on ENSO events lasting at least 5 months [29]. However, the DcSTCA could not entirely identify the southward expanding region of precipitation anomaly over eastern tropical Pacific in November and December of 2010. This was due to the fact that the region expanding to the south was temporary and did not last for more than time interval threshold. Unlike the DcSTCA, the OSCAR does not have this problem, as it only takes into account the time factor when clustering snapshot objects but not consider the time factor when extracting snapshot objects."

Figure 11 depicts the clustering results obtained by DcSTCA with a number threshold of 30 and an attribute threshold of 2.38 in comparison to those of OSCAR. In accordance with the DcSTCA literature, the parameter for time interval threshold was set at 2, given that ENSO events persist for at least five months [29]. However, DcSTCA was unable to fully capture the southward expansion of precipitation anomalies over the eastern tropical Pacific in November and December 2010. This shortfall arose as the southward expansion was temporary and did not exceed the time interval threshold. In contrast, OSCAR circumvents this issue by solely incorporating the time factor during the clustering of snapshot objects, rather than considering it during the extraction of snapshot objects.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "illustrates" to "depicts" | Used a synonym for variety and enhanced readability |
| Changed "achieved by" to "obtained by" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "compared to the OSCAR" to "in comparison to those of OSCAR" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
| Changed "Referring to the literature of" to "In accordance with" | Improved concision and readability |
| Changed "the pamater" to "the parameter" | Corrected spelling error |
| Changed "events lasting" to "events persist" | Used a synonym for variety and enhanced readability |
| Changed "the fact that" to "as" | Improved concision |
| Changed "and did not last" to "and did not exceed" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
| Changed "Unlike the DcSTCA" to "In contrast, OSCAR" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "but not consider the time factor" to "rather than considering it" | Improved concision and readability |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"The relationship between the ENSO index and the precipitation anomaly cluster is shown in Figure 12. In this figure, the precipitation anomaly of node centre represents the mean precipitation anomaly of snapshot objects, and the node’s area corresponds to the pixel count of snapshot object excluding those with smaller than 10 pixels whose size is 1°×1°. The edge between nodes represents the evolution relationship between snapshot objects, such as development, merging and splitting."

Figure 12 illustrates the relationship between the ENSO index and the precipitation anomaly cluster. In this depiction, the precipitation anomaly of the node center represents the mean precipitation anomaly of snapshot objects, while the node's area correlates to the pixel count of the snapshot object, excluding cases smaller than 10 pixels with a size of 1°×1°. The edges between nodes signify the evolutionary relationships among snapshot objects, encompassing development, merging, and splitting processes.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "is shown" to "illustrates" | Enhanced readability and concision |
| Changed "node centre" to "node center" | Americanized spelling for consistency |
| Changed "area corresponds" to "area correlates" | Improved clarity |
| Changed "those with smaller than 10 pixels" to "cases smaller than 10 pixels" | Improved readability |
| Changed "The edge between nodes represents" to "The edges between nodes signify" | Improved clarity and used a synonym for variety |

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper. Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modification and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table: 
"This was due to the fact that the region expanding to the south was temporary and did not last for more than time interval threshold. Unlike the DcSTCA, the OSCAR does not have this problem, as it takes the time factor into account only when clustering snapshot objects and consider spatial and attribute factors instead of time factor when extracting snapshot objects."

This shortfall arose as the southward expansion was temporary and did not exceed the time interval threshold. In contrast, OSCAR circumvents this issue by solely incorporating the time factor during the clustering of snapshot objects, rather than considering it during the extraction of snapshot objects, where spatial and attribute factors are prioritized instead.

| Modification | Reason |
| --- | --- |
| Changed "was due to the fact that" to "arose as" | Improved concision and readability |
| Changed "did not last for more than" to "did not exceed" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
| Changed "the OSCAR does" to "OSCAR" | Improved concision |
| Changed "not have this problem, as" to "circumvents this issue by" | Improved clarity and readability |
| Changed "it takes" to "solely incorporating" | Improved concision and readability |
| Changed "and consider spatial" to "and where spatial" | Improved clarity |
| Changed "instead of time factor" to "are prioritized instead" | Enhanced clarity and readability |
Write a response to the comment of journal reviewer

Dear Reviewer,

Thank you for taking the time to review our manuscript and providing your valuable insights and constructive comments. We truly appreciate your input, which will enable us to improve the quality and clarity of our paper. In response to your comments, we have carefully addressed each point and made appropriate revisions and clarifications where necessary.

We acknowledge your concern about the original conclusion merely repeating the works of this study and lacking a higher level of abstraction. Following your suggestion, we have rewritten the conclusion to emphasize the most important outcomes, provide further perspectives, and discuss the broader implications of our findings in the context of current literature and future research.

We understand the importance of presenting our results in a clear and concise manner while maintaining academic rigor. As such, we have thoroughly proofread and revised the writing throughout the manuscript, with the guiding principles of improving spelling, grammar, concision, and overall readability.

We are confident that the revised manuscript now addresses your concerns, and we hope that our research will contribute to the existing body of knowledge in this field. Once again, we greatly appreciate your valuable feedback and assistance in refining our work.

Sincerely,
[Your Name and Co-authors]
